input_text:
Added an in-process LRU cache to the user service — a Python dictionary with a max size. Seemed efficient since it avoids a Redis network roundtrip on every request.

Problem: we run 8 Gunicorn worker processes. Each process has its own in-process cache, so we effectively have 8 separate caches. When a user updates their profile, we invalidate the cache in the worker that handled the update — but the other 7 workers still have stale data.

We had an incident where a user changed their email address and kept seeing the old email for 20 minutes because 7 of 8 workers were serving stale cached data.

Moved to Redis as the shared cache.

expected_negatives.what: In-process Python LRU cache in a multi-worker Gunicorn setup
expected_negatives.why: Each of 8 worker processes had its own cache — invalidation in one worker didn't propagate to the others. An email-change incident left 7 of 8 workers serving stale data for ~20 minutes. Moved to Redis as a shared cache.

reviewer_notes: Real incident makes the rejection concrete.
